items.prompt: Two ASCII strings should be compared case-insensitively while preserving every character position. Which normalization is appropriate before comparison?

options.text: Convert both strings to the same case, then compare them position by position.
Sort the characters in both strings before comparison.
Remove repeated characters from both strings before comparison.
Convert only the first string to lowercase.

feedback.reason: The normalization must remove only the distinction declared irrelevant by the contract.
feedback.details: Case is irrelevant, but order, positions, and multiplicity remain meaningful. Converting both strings to one case preserves those properties while removing only case differences.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sort_characters: Sorting destroys positional order, which the comparison still requires.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.deduplicate_characters: Removing repeated characters changes multiplicity even though duplicates remain meaningful.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.normalize_one_side: The same normalization must be applied symmetrically to both inputs.

items.prompt: A comparison ignores punctuation and letter case but preserves the order of letters and digits. Which pipeline matches the contract?

options.text: For each input, keep only letters and digits, convert letters to one case, then compare the resulting sequences.
Remove punctuation, sort the remaining characters, then compare.
Remove punctuation and repeated characters, then compare.
Compare only the original string lengths.

feedback.reason: Normalization should remove punctuation and case differences while preserving the remaining sequence.
feedback.details: Filtering and case folding produce a canonical sequence under the stated equivalence rule. Sorting would additionally make order irrelevant, which is not allowed.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sort_after_filter: Sorting changes the order of meaningful characters.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.remove_duplicates: Duplicate letters and digits remain part of the normalized sequence unless the contract says otherwise.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.compare_raw_lengths: Ignored punctuation can change raw lengths without changing normalized equality.

items.prompt: A username comparison ignores leading and trailing spaces but preserves spaces inside the username. Which normalization is correct?

options.text: Remove whitespace only from the beginning and end of both inputs.
Remove every whitespace character from both inputs.
Replace every run of whitespace with one space.
Sort all non-space characters.

feedback.reason: The normalization must match the exact whitespace rule in the contract.
feedback.details: Only surrounding whitespace is irrelevant. Internal spaces still distinguish usernames, so removing or collapsing them would merge inputs that should remain different.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.remove_all_spaces: This removes internal spaces that remain meaningful.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.collapse_words: The contract does not authorize changing internal whitespace runs.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sort_characters: Sorting destroys the sequence order and is unrelated to trimming.

items.prompt: A normalizer removes ignored separators but must preserve repeated meaningful symbols. What should it produce from ["A", "-", "A", "-", "B"] when only "-" is ignored?

options.text: ["A", "A", "B"], but only because normalization always sorts

feedback.reason: Removing ignored separators does not imply removing duplicate meaningful values.
feedback.details: Both occurrences of A remain because A is meaningful and multiplicity is preserved. Only the separator is discarded.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.deduplicate_all: This silently introduces a uniqueness rule that is not part of normalization.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.keep_separators: The contract explicitly declares the separator irrelevant.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sort_output: The resulting order happens to match the input, but normalization does not automatically authorize sorting.

items.prompt: Which properties should a deterministic canonical normalizer satisfy? Select all that apply.

options.text: Apply the same declared equivalence rules to every compared input.
Normalizing an already normalized sequence should not keep changing it.
Preserve order and multiplicity whenever the contract still treats them as meaningful.
Remove every difference between the inputs until they compare equal.

feedback.reason: Canonical normalization is symmetric, stable, and limited to distinctions declared irrelevant.
feedback.details: A valid normalizer maps equivalent inputs to the same representation without erasing meaningful differences. Idempotence prevents the representation from drifting under repeated application.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.remove_every_difference: Normalization must not force unequal inputs to become equal by deleting meaningful distinctions.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.same_rule_both_inputs: Asymmetric normalization can make equality depend on argument order.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.idempotent_result: A canonical representation should already satisfy its own normalization rules.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.preserve_meaningful_data: Information outside the ignored equivalence dimensions must remain intact.

items.prompt: Two product codes should be compared ignoring hyphens and ASCII letter case, but digit order remains significant. Are "ab-12" and "A-B21" equal under this rule?

options.text: No. They normalize to "AB12" and "AB21", which differ in digit order.
Yes. They contain the same letters and digits.
Yes. Sorting the normalized characters makes them equal.
No. The hyphens appear in different positions.

feedback.reason: Only hyphens and case are ignored; the order of retained characters still defines equality.
feedback.details: After filtering and case folding, the digit sequences are 12 and 21. Sorting would add an unstated order-insensitive rule.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.equal_same_characters: Having the same multiset of characters is insufficient when order remains meaningful.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.equal_after_sorting: Sorting changes the contract by discarding positional order.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.not_equal_hyphen_positions: Hyphens are explicitly ignored, so their positions do not affect equality.

items.prompt: Arrange the steps for comparing two sequences under a shared normalization contract.

elements.text: State exactly which differences are irrelevant and which remain meaningful.
Apply the same normalization to both sequences.
Test empty, all-ignored, repeated, and already-normalized inputs.

feedback.reason: The equivalence relation determines the legal normalizer, and the normalized outputs determine equality.
feedback.details: Starting with implementation before defining ignored distinctions risks deleting meaningful information. Boundary tests then verify that the chosen normalizer is stable and complete.

items.prompt: A sequence is normalized in place by removing ignored values and returning a logical length k. Which statement is correct?

options.text: The normalized result is the prefix [0, k); the suffix may remain outside the logical contract.
Every position after k must be cleared for the result to be valid.
The physical container length must always shrink to k.
Any repeated retained values must also be removed.

feedback.reason: In-place compaction commonly defines a logical result prefix rather than a rewritten suffix.
feedback.details: The returned k identifies the valid normalized sequence. Clearing or resizing the remainder is unnecessary unless explicitly required. Repeated meaningful values remain repeated.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.suffix_must_be_zeroed: The suffix is outside the logical result unless the specification says otherwise.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.physical_length_required: Logical length and physical storage length are separate concepts.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.duplicates_removed_automatically: Filtering ignored values does not introduce an unstated deduplication rule.

items.prompt: A comparison ignores character order but requires exact multiplicity. Which normalized representation is sufficient for lowercase ASCII strings?

options.text: A 26-entry frequency vector for each string.
A set of characters present in each string.
The distinct characters in sorted order.
Only the first character of each string.

feedback.reason: When order is irrelevant but multiplicity matters, counts form the canonical representation.
feedback.details: Equal frequency vectors mean every character occurs the same number of times. A set or unique-character sequence loses multiplicity.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.seen_set: Presence cannot distinguish one occurrence from several.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sorted_unique_characters: Removing duplicate occurrences loses required count information.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.first_character: One position cannot summarize the full character multiset.

items.prompt: Which cases should be included when reviewing a filter-and-compare normalizer? Select all that apply.

options.text: Inputs whose characters are all ignored by the normalization rule.
Inputs already in canonical form.
Inputs containing repeated meaningful values.
Only two ordinary inputs that already compare equal.

feedback.reason: Boundary tests should verify empty canonical output, idempotence, and preservation of meaningful multiplicity.
feedback.details: All-ignored inputs test empty results. Already-normalized inputs test stability. Repeated meaningful values catch accidental deduplication.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.only_happy_path: A single ordinary equal case does not exercise the failure boundaries of normalization.

omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.all_ignored: The normalizer must handle a canonical result of length zero.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.already_normalized: Canonical input should remain unchanged under repeated normalization.
omittedCorrectExplanationsByOptionId.repeated_meaningful: The normalizer must not delete multiplicity that remains part of the contract.

items.prompt: A developer lowercases the first input but leaves the second unchanged before a case-insensitive comparison. What is the defect?

options.text: The normalization is asymmetric, so equivalent inputs can compare differently depending on argument order.
Lowercasing is never a valid normalization.
Every normalized comparison must sort both inputs.
The only defect is that duplicate counts were not computed.

feedback.reason: Both sides of an equivalence comparison must be mapped through the same canonicalization rule.
feedback.details: For example, lowercase("A") equals "a", but lowercase("a") compared with raw "A" fails. The outcome should not depend on which input is placed first.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.lowercase_always_invalid: Lowercasing is valid when ASCII case is explicitly irrelevant.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.comparison_requires_sorting: Sorting is needed only when order is also irrelevant.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.duplicate_counts_missing: Count state is unrelated to the asymmetry defect in this case.

items.prompt: A normalizer removes every zero from an integer sequence before comparison. Why might this be incorrect?

options.text: Zero may be a meaningful value unless the comparison contract explicitly declares it ignorable.
A linear scan cannot process zero values.
Normalization is valid only when the input contains duplicates.
Removing zeros always takes O(n²) time.

feedback.reason: Normalization rules come from semantic equivalence, not from values that appear convenient to discard.
feedback.details: If zero contributes to the sequence meaning, removing it merges distinct inputs. A value may be ignored only when the contract explicitly defines that difference as irrelevant.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.zeros_cannot_be_scanned: Zero is processed like any other integer value.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.normalization_requires_duplicates: Duplicate presence is unrelated to whether a value is ignorable.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.zero_removal_always_quadratic: Stable filtering can be performed in one linear scan.

items.prompt: Two normalized sequences are compared position by position after each input was scanned once. If their lengths are n and m before normalization, which time bound is precise?
constraints: Each input is normalized in one pass.
Comparing canonical outputs is linear in their retained lengths.

feedback.reason: Each original input may need to be fully examined before canonical equality is known.
feedback.details: The two normalization passes cost O(n) and O(m). Comparing outputs costs at most O(n + m) in total, so the combined bound remains O(n + m).

items.prompt: A comparison requires original order after ignored characters are removed. Why is sorting the normalized outputs unsafe?

options.text: Sorting can make different meaningful sequences appear equal by erasing their relative order.
Sorting cannot be applied to sequence elements.
Sorting always removes repeated values.
Sorting is unsafe because it always uses O(1) space.

feedback.reason: A canonicalization step may not discard order when order remains part of equality.
feedback.details: For example, AB and BA become the same sorted sequence even though their normalized orders differ. Sorting is legal only when order is explicitly irrelevant.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sorting_cannot_handle_strings: Sequences can be sorted; the issue is semantic legality, not technical possibility.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sorting_removes_duplicates: Sorting rearranges elements but does not itself deduplicate them.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sorting_requires_constant_space: Space usage is not the contract violation described here.

items.prompt: A whitespace normalizer collapses every run of spaces to one space. For which contract is this normalization legal?

options.text: When the number of consecutive spaces is irrelevant but word separation remains meaningful.
When every original space position and count must be preserved.
When spaces must be removed rather than retained as separators.
Whenever character order is irrelevant.

feedback.reason: Collapsing runs preserves separation while discarding only whitespace multiplicity.
feedback.details: It is correct only when one or many consecutive spaces represent the same boundary. Other contracts may require exact spaces or no spaces at all.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.all_spaces_meaningful: Collapsing would destroy required whitespace positions and multiplicities.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.spaces_removed_entirely: Keeping one separator does not satisfy a contract that removes spaces entirely.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.order_irrelevant: Order relevance does not determine how whitespace runs should be normalized.

items.prompt: A record comparison ignores the case of ASCII letters in a name field but treats digits, punctuation, order, and duplicate characters as meaningful. Which implementation best matches the contract?

options.text: Convert letters in both names to one case and compare the complete resulting sequences.
Convert case, remove punctuation, and compare.
Convert case, sort characters, remove duplicates, and compare.
Compare only the sets of distinct characters.

feedback.reason: The implementation should normalize case and preserve every other declared distinction.
feedback.details: Digits, punctuation, sequence order, and multiplicity remain meaningful. Removing, sorting, or deduplicating them would broaden the equivalence relation beyond the specification.

wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.remove_punctuation: Punctuation remains meaningful under this contract.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.sort_and_deduplicate: Sorting and deduplication erase order and multiplicity that must be preserved.
wrongOptionExplanationsByOptionId.compare_distinct_sets: A set loses both order and repeated occurrences.
